coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a wide range of applications in biomedical applications, particularly anticancer drug delivery. In this study, graphene oxide quantum dots conjugated with glucosamine and boric acid (GOQDs-GlcN-BA) with and without doxorubicin (DOX) were investigated for cancer therapy for the first time in the literature. The nanocomposite contained 4 w/w boron according to the ICP analysis. Furthermore, the size of the nanocomposites ranged between 15 and 51 nm. DOX loading efficiencies of GOQDs-GlcN-DOX and GOQDs-GlcN-BA-DOX were 57% and 90%, respectively. Only 8% of DOX release occurred from GOQDs-GlcN-DOX in acidic environment and 5% of drug released at neutral pH, whereas BA addition in the nanocomposite increased DOX release up to 20% at 5.5 pH and 10% at 7.4 pH in 96 h. GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX exhibited more cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells compared to GOQD-GlcN-DOX. Cellular internalization of GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX in MCF-7 was higher than it was observed for GOQDs-GlcN-DOX owing to sialic acid-BA interaction on cell surface. This work shows the incorporation of BA to the nanocomposite enhanced the loading and release of DOX, with higher cellular internalization, thus superior potential for anticancer drug delivery.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a wide range of applications in biomedical applications, particularly anticancer drug delivery. In this study, graphene oxide quantum dots conjugated with glucosamine and boric acid (GOQDs-GlcN-BA) with and without doxorubicin (DOX) were investigated for cancer therapy for the first time in the literature. The nanocomposite contained 4 w/w boron according to the ICP analysis. Furthermore, the size of the nanocomposites ranged between 15 and 51 nm. DOX loading efficiencies of GOQDs-GlcN-DOX and GOQDs-GlcN-BA-DOX were 57% and 90%, respectively. Only 8% of DOX release occurred from GOQDs-GlcN-DOX in acidic environment and 5% of drug released at neutral pH, whereas BA addition in the nanocomposite increased DOX release up to 20% at 5.5 pH and 10% at 7.4 pH in 96 h. GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX exhibited more cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells compared to GOQD-GlcN-DOX. Cellular internalization of GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX in MCF-7 was higher than it was observed for GOQDs-GlcN-DOX owing to sialic acid-BA interaction on cell surface. This work shows the incorporation of BA to the nanocomposite enhanced the loading and release of DOX, with higher cellular internalization, thus superior potential for anticancer drug delivery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene is an ultra-thin two-dimensional structure composed of sp2-bonded carbon atoms that forms a honeycomb crystal lattice [1]. It is made up of a single atom and a thick planar sheet [2]. GO is a graphene derivative with a single atomic layered material, and its surface is functionalized with a large number of residual epoxide (-O-), hydroxide (-OH), and carboxylic acid (-COOH) groups attached on the basal planes and edges [3]. These polar groups increase the surface energy of GO for improved hydrophilicity while enabling functionalization through various chemical modifications [4,5].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), one of graphene's derivatives, are carbon-based nanomaterials made up of a single layer of 2D graphene [6]. Quantum dots are used to reduce the size of the sheets to less than 100 nm [7], showing better properties with more active groups on their surface than normal graphene and GO. Main characteristics of GQDs are their water solubility, non-toxicity, inexpensiveness, good biocompatibility, large surface area, small size, photostability and better cellular uptake. Additionally, they have fluorescence property due to the optical bandgap open by the sp2 and sp3 carbons which helps in bioimaging without the need for external dyes [8] [–] [10]. Moreover, strong non-covalent interactions with adsorbed biomolecules occur on the 2D surface via π-π interactions, electrostatic forces, or hydrogen bonding [11–13]. Research on drug delivery and imaging applications of GQDs is widespread [14]. Chitosan modified graphene quantum dots have been developed for the delivery of cytarabine an anticancer drug. The system showed a controlled pH sensitive drug release due to the hydrolysis of chitosan amide linkage. The presence of chitosan gel enhanced the fluorescent stability of GQDs [15]. DOX loaded Fe3O4@PEG decorated GQD have also been reported. The loading efficiency of DOX drug was 71%. The study succeeded in controllable magnetic release of DOX from the synthesized non-toxic nanocarrier based on graphene quantum dot decorated with magnetic nanoparticles [16].

Boric acid (BA) is one boron atom surrounded by three oxygen atoms forming triangle groups linked with hydrogen bonds BO3. The structure of BA clusters behaves similarly to carbon clusters [17]. BA and many of its derivatives can react with the diols in cell glycocalyx, which resulted in good cell permeability due to actively crossing the cell membrane [18,19]. BA is selective to cancer cells due to highly expressed sialic acid by many cancer cells (colon, breast, cervix, choriocarcinomas and acute myeloid leukemia) [17,20,21].

Although the recent years have seen the development of many innovative nanocarriers for anticancer drug delivery; safety, effectiveness and cost are still an important bottleneck before translation into clinic. Nano-delivery systems lack the necessary level of physiological stability, biocompatibility or effective targeting on cancer cells to minimize systemic toxicity [22–24].

In this study, we investigated GOQD-GlcN-BA nanocomposite as a nanocarrier for DOX delivery for the first time. We hypothesized that BA complexation would increase DOX loading efficiency and anticancer effect through higher cell internalization without any unspecific toxicity. GOQD was covalently bound to GlcN and complexed with BA before DOX loading via non-covalent interactions for the first time in the literature (Scheme 1 ). The drug release profile in acidic and physiological pH environment, cytotoxicity toward MCF-7 breast cancer cell line and cellular internalization were explored for nanocomposites GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA.

Materials used for synthesis of GO; Graphite powder (Average size: 3 mm, Ege Nanotek, Turkey), potassium permanganate (KMnO4) (ISOLAB, Germany), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), phosphoric acid (H3PO4) (ISOLAB, Germany), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (ISOLAB, Germany), hydrochloric acid (HCl) (Merck, Germany). N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) (Sigma-Aldrich, USA), 2-(N-Morpholino) ethanesulfonic acid (MES).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of graphene oxide
ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized by Tour method [25]. Firstly, 3 g of graphite powder was mixed with 18 g of potassium permanganate (KMnO4) in glass beaker. Secondly, 360 ml of sulfuric acid (H2SO4) was mixed with 40 ml (75%) phosphoric acid (H3PO4) solution to obtain a volume ratio of 9:1 in the solution. Then, the solution was added into powder mixture while stirring, during which a temperature increase up to 45 °C was observed. The solution was stirred at 50 °C for 12 h. After 12 h of stirring, heating was stopped and color of the solution changed from greenish to purple-brown. Then, 400 ml of dH2O ice cubes were added to the solution to stop the reaction. Afterwards, 3 ml hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) solution was added to the mixture after ice melted, and the color changed to light yellow, which is an indication of GO formation. After that, tap water was added. The mixture was left for 3 days until all the GO settled down, then the solution was taken out. Afterwards, the precipitate was washed 3 times with dH2O, one time with HCl (30%) and again 3 times with dH2O. Finally, the precipitate was collected by centrifugation (5000 g for 8 min) (Hettich EBA 20, UK) and homogenized at 12,000 r/min. Obtained GO was characterized by FTIR, XRD and SEM. Details of the protocol are given in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: To synthesize GOQDs, 24 mg of GO was dispersed in 3 ml at 80 °C, and 40 ml of H2O2 (30%) was added. Afterwards, 7 ml of ammonia (25–28%) was gradually added to the mixture and the entire mixture was left for 24 h with vigorous stirring to obtain a light-yellow transparent solution. To remove large particles, a 0.22 μm membrane was used [26]. The quantum dots were lyophilized for 3 days before being dispersed in 24 ml of MES buffer (0.1 M, pH 6) at the concentration of 1 mg/ml.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of glucose amine (GlcN) on GOQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 20 ml of GOQDs mixture (1 mg/ml) was added to 10 ml of MES buffer (0.1 M, pH 6) containing 13.3 mg of glucosamine. 320 mg of EDC and 106.6 mg of NHS were each dissolved in 5 ml of MES buffer separately, then slowly added to the GOQDs-GlcN solution. The reaction was completed after 20 h, filtered using an Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (3 kDa). After that the solution was lyophilized for 2 days [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GOQD-GlcN with boric acid (BA)
ce:section.ce:para: Sodium bicarbonate and sodium carbonate were used to prepare the bicarbonate buffer. In 5 ml dH2O, 315 mg of sodium bicarbonate (0.747 M) and 44 mg of sodium carbonate (0.083 M) were dissolved and the pH was adjusted to pH 9.3.4 ml of GOQD-GlcN was mixed with 0.6 ml of bicarbonate buffer. An excess of BA, 500 mg in 1 ml of dH2O, was then added to the mixture. Then, 5 ml of the mixture was kept under stirring for 24 h. The nanocomposite was dialyzed for 24 h against NaCl (1 M) solution to increase the ionic strength of the solution in order to remove BA bound by ionic interactions instead of those formed a stable complex with the nanocomposite [27], and then dialyzed against dH2O to remove BA and NaCl. Dialysis was performed using an Amicon ultra-centrifugal filter (MW cut-off: 3 kDa) [27].

The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) were used to identify the bonds formed on all synthesized nanomaterials (GOQD, GOQD-GlcN, GOQD-GlcN-BA). For FTIR analysis, the compound system consisting of a spectrometer and a microscope (Bruker IFS 66/S and Hyperion 1000, Germany) was used. The samples were examined in the mid-infrared (MIR) range (between 4000 and 400 cm−1).

TEM was used to determine the morphology and size of the quantum dots (FEI Tecnai G2 Spirit BioTwin CTEM, Sweden). Before analysis, the samples were dispersed in dH2O and transferred onto 300-mesh carbon film coated copper grids. The size of the nanoassemblies was measured by Image J program. The area of the particles was obtained from TEM images and then the diameters of 50 particles from each nanoassembly were calculated.

Zeta potential of the nanocomposites was determined by Laser Doppler Electrophoresis using zeta sizer NanoZS (Malvern Panalytical, UK) to assess the surface charge of GOQD, GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA.
ICP-MS analysis (PerkinElmer Plasma 400, USA) was used to determine the elemental composition of graphene oxide and show the presence of boron in the quantum dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading into GOQD-GlcN and GOQD- GlcN -BA
ce:section.ce:para:
For DOX loading, 0.45 ml of 1.2 mg of the nanocomposite suspension was dispersed in 0.85 ml dH2O. Afterwards, 0.2 ml of 2 mg/ml DOX was then added and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Drug to carrier ratio was one-to-three (w/w). After 24 h, unbound DOX was removed by dialysis using dialysis tubing with a cutoff 3.5 kDa. After 8 and 24 h, the medium was refreshed with 5 ml PBS. The fluorescence intensity of the dialysates was measured at 490 nm (excitation wavelength) and 600 nm (emission wavelength) via microplate spectrophotometer (μOuantTM, Biotek Instruments Inc., USA) and the total amount of unbound DOX was determined using the calibration curve constructed with different concentrations of DOX in PBS. The loading efficiency and drug loading capacity of GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA were determined using Eqs. (1) and (2).

DOX release from the nanocomposites GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA was studied at 37 °C in two different recipient media: pH 5.5 (citric buffer saline) and pH 7.4 (phosphate buffer saline). 0.6 mg of the sample was placed in a dialysis tubing and incubated in 3 ml media. At various time intervals (1, 2, 6, 8, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h), aliquots (0.1 ml) from the release media were withdrawn and replaced with same volume of fresh medium. The fluorescence of the supernatant was measured (excitation wavelength of 485 nm and emission wavelength of 610 nm). To compute the percentage of DOX release Eq. (3) was used. The release data are given as cumulative percent release.

MCF-7 cells were cultured in media composed of 10% FBS, 1% penicillin/streptomycin, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 88% DMEM.

Cytotoxicity study was performed to see the effect of different concentrations of the synthesized quantum dots on MCF-7 breast cancer cells. MCF-7 cells were seeded in 96 well plates (4
×
103 cells/well) and cultured in a DMEM cell culture medium for 5 h under humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 – 95% air in incubator (SHEL LAB, USA). The cells were then treated for 24 h in a DMEM medium with various concentrations of GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25 and 50 μg/ml), and GOQD-GlcN-DOX, GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX and only DOX (0, 2, 4, 8,16, 32 μg/ml) [26]. After that, the medium was discarded and the cells were washed with 150 μl of PBS, to remove the physically adsorbed nanomaterials before adding MTT solution. 100 μl of fresh DMEM and 10 μl of MTT solution were added (5 mg/ml, 10 μl) to each well and the cells were incubated for a 4 h. Finally, the medium was discarded and 50 μl of DMSO was added to dissolve formazan violet crystals after the medium was removed. A microplate reader was used to measure the absorbance of solubilized formazan at 570 nm.

The % viability of the cells was calculated by the optical density (ODs) Eq. (4)

The morphology of the cells was visualized after 24 h of incubation. After 24 h, the cells were washed with PBS, fixed with 100 μl of paraformaldehyde (4%) for 15 min, and then washed again. Phase-contrast images of the cells were taken using phase-contrast microscope (Nikon Eclipse TS 100).

MCF-7 cells were used to study the cellular internalization of DOX, GOQD-GlcN-DOX and GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX. Cells were seeded onto 24 well plate (17 × 103 cells/well) and cultured for 24 h. After 24 h, the cells were washed with PBS and then incubated with DOX (33.7 μg/ml), GOQD-GlcN-DOX and GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX at 37 °C for 1, 8 and 24 h. After incubation, the cells were washed with PBS and fixed with 200 μl paraformaldehyde (4%) for 20 min. Cells were examined with confocal microscopy and photographed. The quantum dots were excited with 488 nm laser and the emission was collected from 500 nm to 530 nm. DOX was excited with 488 nm laser and the emission signal was collected from 552 nm to 617 nm [8].

To compare groups for single parameter, one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) test was applied. Tukey's Multiple Comparison Test for the post-hoc pairwise comparisons (SPSS-22 Software Programme, SPPS Inc., USA) was used; p < 0.05 was considered as statistically significant.

Oxidation of graphite produces GO, which contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups. There are many methods reported in the literature for GO synthesis and Hummer's method is the most commonly used [28]. We preferred Tour's method, which is a modification over Hummer's method that yields GO with more regular structure and higher oxidation due to the utilization of phosphoric acid during synthesis [28]. These groups are depicted in the FTIR spectra (Fig. S1). A broad band at 3196 cm−1 is attributed to O–H groups and the peak for C–H stretching can be seen at 2917 cm−1. The stretching vibrations of CO at 1729 cm−1 and CC at 1616 cm−1 were also observed [29]. Epoxy stretching, C–O, O–H deformation and alkoxy stretching were assigned to the peaks 1220 cm−1, 1373 cm−1, and 1040 cm−1 [30], respectively. The presence of C–O–C groups was evident with peak observed at 1159 cm−1 [31].

The crystal structure of GO was analyzed by XRD (Fig. S2). Strong peak at 2Ө = 10.62° with (001) plane occurred with an interlayer spacing of 8.3 Ȧ corresponding to the presence of oxidized graphite layers was observed.

The surface morphology of GO was studied with SEM. The surface contained large wrinkles due to the agglomeration of the sheets, similar to the other reports in the literature [32,33]. Fig. S3 shows the bulk size of GO after drying, with sheets stacking on top of one another to form a thick sheet [34].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GOQD, GOQD-GlcN and GOQD- GlcN -BA
ce:section.ce:para: GOQD was synthesized via top-down route by oxidation of GO with H2O2. In this process, ammonia was also used concurrently with H2O2 in order to control the synthesis by stabilizing the structure by amidation reaction [35]. Afterwards, the synthesized GOQD was conjugated with GlcN via EDC-NHS (molar ratio: 2.2) coupling in pH 6 buffer. Finally, BA complexation was achieved in a bicarbonate buffer system. BA has a high pKa, which limits its dissociation in neutral and low pH environments. As a result, a bicarbonate buffer with a pH of 9 is required to allow the interaction [36].

The FTIR spectra of GOQD, GOQD-GlcN, GlcN, GOQD-GlcN-BA, BA are illustrated in Fig. 1 a. Characteristic large number of oxygen functional groups of GOQD characterized are shown in the spectrum. The peak at 3423 cm−1 corresponds to the –OH. The stretching vibrations of CO of carboxylic acid groups are observed at 1748 cm−1, and CC are represented by peaks at 1616 cm−1. The peak at 1381 cm−1 represents stretching vibration of C–O of the carboxyl group. Asymmetric stretching of the C–O–C bridge is observed in the absorption band at 1123 cm−1. The conjugation of GlcN to GOQD was confirmed with the peaks at 1635 cm−1 and 1554 cm−1, which are ascribed to NH–CO and N–H groups respectively [26],resulting in the disappearance of the carboxylic acid peak. The peak at 1388 cm−1 represents the B–O [37].

The morphology and size of the QDs (GOQD, GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA) were investigated with TEM (Fig. 1b). GOQD and GOQD-GlcN showed spherical shapes, similar previous reports [38], whereas GOQD-GlcN-BA resembled polygonal shapes rather than spherical.

Size distribution of nanocomposites demonstrate a shift in frequency towards greater particle diameters after each incorporation step (Fig. 2 ) [39]. Average particle diameters of GOQD, GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA were 16 nm, 27 nm and 51 nm, respectively. Similar sized GOQD [40], GOQD-GlcN particles can be found in the literature. Greater size of GOQD-GlcN-BA indicated the successful complexation of BA on GOQD-GlcN while complying well with other studies [16,41–43], supporting its suitability for cellular uptake as a nanoscale drug carrier [16,44,45].

Surface charge of the nanocomposites was determined by zeta potential analysis. GOQD, GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA were characterized with negative surface charge. Consistent with other reports [13,46], GOQD had a negative charge of −16.5 mV. Conjugation of GlcN to GOQD decreased the negativity of the surface charge as the negatively charged functional groups on GOQD were covered during the conjugation. However, BA complexation onto GOQD-GlcN changed the net charge from −9.6 mV to −11.23 mV, slightly more negative due to the anionic nature of BA (Fig. 3 ) [27].
The weight percent of boron in the nanocomposite GOQD-GlcN-BA was determined by ICP as 4 w/w, as compared to 9 w/w of boron obtained in another study [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and drug release results
ce:section.ce:para: QDs, in general, are characterized with many properties such as tunable size, high surface area, stability and optical properties [47]. GOQDs have similar properties as GO and similarly they can be functionalized. Furthermore, the abundance of oxygen functional groups contributes to its solubility and stability. GOQDs can also form hydrophobic interactions and π-π stack with aromatic drugs [26].

DOX loaded into GOQDs-GlcN at 1:3 ratio resulted in 57% drug loading efficiency and 19 wt% loading capacity. GOQDs-GlcN and BA loaded with DOX with the same ratio (1:3) had much higher loading efficiency (around 90%) and loading capacity (30 wt%) compared to GOQD-GlcN. This increase in loading efficiency and loading capacity can be attributed to the fact that the complexed BA can form pH-dependent reversible ester bonds with 1,3-diol containing compounds such as DOX [48,49].
Table 1 compares the loading capacity and loading efficiency of DOX in various nanocarriers found in the literature with the results of this study.

The release study was conducted with free DOX, GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA for 96 h at neutral pH 7.4 and acidic pH 5.5. While free DOX in pH 7.4 was released (from dialysis tubing) by 86%, increased solubility of DOX in acidic medium increased its release to 89% in 24 h. GOQDs-GlcN proved its pH sensitivity through higher release observed at pH 5.5. Cumulative DOX release after 96 h at neutral pH was around 5.7%, whereas in acidic medium the release went up to 8.6%. Similarly, higher release rate was observed with GOQD-GlcN-BA at pH 5.5 (20%) compared to pH 7.4 (around 10%) as illustrated in Fig. 4 . The pH sensitive release might be due to the NH2 groups on DOX which becomes protonated in acidic solutions to facilitate dissociation of hydrogen-bonding interactions as well as the pH dependent interactions between BA and DOX [54]. Overall, GOQD-GlcN-BA demonstrated excellent DOX loading efficiency and loading capacity in addition to pH-dependent and sustainable release profile in comparison to many other nanocomposites for DOX delivery [55–57].

Relative viability of MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of GOQD-GlcN & GOQD-GlcN-BA was determined after 24 h of incubation (Fig. 5 ). No cytotoxicity was observed for any of the tested concentrations. Cells incubated in media containing GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA showed a relative cell viability slightly above 100%, without significant difference between two groups (Fig. 5a). In addition to the cytocompatibility GOQDs [58], presence of glucosamine might have further enhanced the cellular viability [59]. The induction of GLUT1 and SGL1 by the tumor cells enhances glucose uptake, thus increase glycolysis as glucose uptake increased [60]. Therefore, both nanocomposite groups have excellent cytocompatibility and other reports also showed good results for nanocomposites incorporating GlcN [29] and BA [61].

Morphology of the cells is shown in Fig. 5b. For the tested concentrations ranging from 6.25 μg/ml to 50 μg/ml, typical morphology of MCF-7 cells did not change as compared to control group, which further confirms that GOQD-GlcN and GOQD-GlcN-BA are non-toxic nanocarriers.

To evaluate the anticancer potential of the nanocomposites, viability of MCF-7 cells was determined after treating with different concentrations (2, 4, 8, 16 and 32 μg/ml) of GOQD-GlcN-DOX and GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX. Relative cell viability with respect to control group (cells cultured in medium only) and only DOX group (cells treated with DOX only) was determined after 24 h of incubation. Viability of the cells treated with GOQD-GlcN-DOX and GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX decreased as the concentration of DOX was increased. The nanocomposite containing BA showed lower cell viability compared to the nanocomposite without BA as the concentration was increased (Fig. 6 a). This could be due to the sialic acid on cell membrane which are highly expressed by MCF-7 cancer cells, which resulted in an interaction between BA and diol groups to result in active transport through the cell membrane [17]. For the highest concentration (32 μg/ml), free DOX showed the highest decrease in cell viability compared to the nanocomposites. This might be ascribed to the fact that while free DOX can readily and unselectively diffuse through cell membranes, the nanocomposites gradually release DOX (Fig. 4) after cellular internalization via endocytosis. A similar result was also observed with curcumin loaded GlcN conjugated graphene quantum dots [26] and DOX loaded MgAl-layered double hydroxide modified Mn3O4/N-graphene quantum dot conjugated-polyaniline [41].

Fig. 6b shows the morphology of the cells treated with nanocomposite loaded with the drug. It was clearly observed that as the concentration of DOX loaded increased, the number of dead cells increased while other cells demonstrated more and more altered morphology.

In vitro tests on MCF-7 cells showed that GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX was more cytotoxic compared to GOQD-GlcN-DOX. Confocal microscopy analysis revealed higher cell internalization of GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX compared to GOQD-GlcN-DOX. This could be explained by the selectivity of BA to sialic acid overexpressed by MCF-7 cancer cells which resulted in good cellular permeability (Scheme 2 ) [17,62].

Thus GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX exhibited much better cellular uptake as shown in Fig. 7 , even after 8 h of incubation. Other nanocarriers taking the advantage of BA-sialic acid interaction on cancer cells to reduce non-specific uptake of cancer drugs by cells were recently reported in the literature with conforming results [63–65].

For the first time in the literature, GOQDs were functionalized with glucosamine and boric acid, resulting in excellent drug delivery properties. DOX was loaded onto the nanocomposites GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX to kill MCF-7 breast cancer cells. The addition of boric acid to GOQD-GlcN increased the loading and release percentages of DOX (loading efficiency of up to 90% and release percentage of up to 20% in acidic environment). The novel GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX nanocomposite exceeded GOQD-GlcN-DOX in terms of cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells. Furthermore, GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX had higher cellular internalization via sialic acid and BA interaction in in-vitro cellular uptake studies. The newly synthesized nanocomposite GOQD-GlcN-BA-DOX has a potential for anticancer drug delivery applications due to the controlled drug release, non-toxicity of the nano-assembly, and increased cellular internalization.
